name: gold ring with red stone
shortDescription: A gold ring set with a red stone, bright enough to look bold even on a careful hand. It carries the old language of vows, victories, and expensive apologies, where the wearer wants warmth to be noticed before words begin.
flavorText: “Red stone on gold is a promise with its lantern lit.”

name: gold ring with white stone
shortDescription: A gold ring holding a white stone, clean and formal in the way temple steps are clean after rain. It is often chosen for oaths, inheritances, and ceremonies where wealth is expected to look almost innocent.
flavorText: “White stone gives gold permission to behave solemnly.”

name: gold ring with yellow stone
shortDescription: A gold ring set with a yellow stone that catches light like trapped honey. It is a cheerful, prosperous little jewel, best suited to festival hands, generous gifts, and merchants who enjoy looking lucky.
flavorText: “Yellow stone makes gold smile.”

name: market granite ring
shortDescription: A granite ring from a market tray, heavier in character than in price. Its speckled stone has a stubborn, earthy honesty to it, the sort of charm bought by people who trust rocks more than promises.
flavorText: “Granite wears poorly at court and wonderfully in real life.”

name: market iron ring
shortDescription: A market iron ring, plain, dark, and practical enough to look more like a tool than an ornament. It suits working hands, sworn guards, and anyone who would rather be thought reliable than decorative.
flavorText: “Iron rings do not flatter the hand; they test it.”

name: ivory ring
shortDescription: An ivory ring with a soft pale sheen and the faint warmth of old craft. It looks gentle from a distance, but up close it carries the uneasy luxury of something once living made into status.
flavorText: “Ivory never quite forgets that it was not born for fingers.”

name: jade ring
shortDescription: A jade ring with a cool green body, smooth enough to make the thumb return to it again and again. Traders value it for beauty, yes, but also for the way old families speak of jade as if it listens.
flavorText: “Jade is not worn quickly. It must be kept company.”

name: moonstone ring
shortDescription: A moonstone ring with a pale inner shimmer that seems to shift whenever the hand moves. It is a favorite of night travelers, shy lovers, and fortune-tellers who prefer their tools to look like quiet weather.
flavorText: “Moonstone does not shine; it remembers light.”

name: opal ring
shortDescription: An opal ring alive with small hidden colors, modest from one angle and extravagant from the next. It rewards restless fingers and suspicious eyes, which is why buyers either adore it or distrust it immediately.
flavorText: “Opal is the only stone that looks like it is keeping several opinions.”

name: pearl ring
shortDescription: A pearl ring with a smooth sea-born glow, softer than cut gems and more intimate than bright metal. It suggests tide gifts, careful courtship, and the quiet confidence of beauty that was never sharpened.
flavorText: “A pearl asks the light to be gentle.”

name: market plain black ring
shortDescription: A plain black ring from the market, simple enough to be overlooked and striking enough to be chosen twice. It is bought for mourning, fashion, secrecy, or simply because not every hand wants to glitter.
flavorText: “Black rings are for people who know silence has a color.”

name: plain blue ring
shortDescription: A plain blue ring, modest in make but pleasant in color, like a small piece of fair weather carried on the hand. It has the charm of an affordable gift chosen with care rather than calculation.
flavorText: “Blue need not be precious to be remembered.”

name: plain green ring
shortDescription: A plain green ring with an easy springtime look, simple enough for daily wear and bright enough to please. It belongs on hands that plant, cook, mend, bargain, or reach for life without ceremony.
flavorText: “Green rings are small promises that winter is not permanent.”

name: plain magenta ring
shortDescription: A plain magenta ring with more personality than cost, bold against the skin and nearly impossible to ignore. It is the sort of market trinket bought by someone in a mood they intend to keep.
flavorText: “Magenta does not ask whether it matches.”

name: plain red ring
shortDescription: A plain red ring, bright, direct, and a little theatrical despite its humble make. It is worn for luck, affection, defiance, and the useful feeling that one small color can change an entire day.
flavorText: “A red ring is a tiny shout the hand can afford.”

name: plain yellow ring
shortDescription: A plain yellow ring with the sunny confidence of a festival stall. It is not trying to look noble; it is trying to look cheerful, which in a hard market may be the rarer achievement.
flavorText: “Yellow rings sell best when the weather has been miserable.”

name: fabled ring of death
shortDescription: A fabled ring of death, cold in reputation even before it is touched. Whether cursed, ceremonial, or merely surrounded by terrible stories, it has the sort of value that makes buyers whisper and sellers avoid guarantees.
flavorText: “Some rings mark a promise. This one marks an ending.”

name: scarce ring of fire
shortDescription: A scarce ring of fire, warm-toned and restless, with metalwork that seems eager for candlelight. It appeals to duelists, hearth-mages, and people who should probably not be trusted near dry curtains.
flavorText: “Fire rings are never blamed for pride, only for what pride touched.”

name: scarce ring of power
shortDescription: A scarce ring of power with a presence larger than its circle, the kind of jewel that makes even sensible buyers lower their voices. It promises nothing plainly, which is exactly why ambitious hands keep reaching for it.
flavorText: “Power rarely announces itself. It waits to be worn.”

name: fabled ring of time
shortDescription: A fabled ring of time, etched with marks too fine to be simple decoration and too regular to be harmless. Clockmakers admire it, priests distrust it, and merchants know any object tied to time eventually becomes a dispute about debt.
flavorText: “Time is the only creditor who never miscounts.”

name: fabled ring of vision
shortDescription: A fabled ring of vision, made for seeing what ordinary eyes miss and perhaps what they were spared from seeing. Its stone seems clear until inspected too long, at which point reflections begin to feel less obedient.
flavorText: “Second sight is still sight; it can still be fooled by wanting.”

name: twisted ring
shortDescription: A twisted ring, bent or forged into a shape that refuses perfect symmetry. It has the look of an old mistake turned into style, which makes it strangely popular with people who dislike neat endings.
flavorText: “Not every crooked thing is broken.”

name: ruby ring
shortDescription: A ruby ring with a hot red heart, small enough to sit politely and bright enough to betray every attempt at modesty. It is a classic choice for love, rank, and quarrels disguised as gifts.
flavorText: “A ruby ring says affection, wealth, and warning in the same breath.”

name: sapphire ring
shortDescription: A sapphire ring with a cool blue face, dignified enough for court and durable enough for daily pride. It flatters hands that sign contracts, pour tea, hold reins, or pretend not to tremble.
flavorText: “Sapphire makes restraint look expensive.”

name: fabled ring of shadows
shortDescription: A fabled ring of shadows, dark without being dull, as if a sliver of lampless hallway had been set into metal. It is coveted by spies, stage magicians, and anyone who believes being unseen is a form of wealth.
flavorText: “A shadow ring is most dangerous when no one compliments it.”

name: silver ring
shortDescription: A simple silver ring, bright, cool, and honest enough to suit almost any hand. It is a good ring for promises made early, wages saved carefully, and gifts that need not shout to be kept.
flavorText: “Silver remembers the moon and the pawnshop equally.”

name: sparkling silver stone ring
shortDescription: A silver ring set with a sparkling stone, lively under lamplight and kinder to the purse than its glitter suggests. It is built for celebration without inheritance, which is sometimes the better kind of joy.
flavorText: “Not every sparkle has to come with a family argument.”

name: market steel ring
shortDescription: A market steel ring, clean-edged and practical, with a shine that feels earned rather than inherited. It appeals to guards, smiths, sailors, and anyone who wants an ornament that can survive a dropped crate.
flavorText: “Steel is not romantic until it stays.”

name: notable tiger eye ring
shortDescription: A notable tiger eye ring with golden-brown bands that shift like a watchful glance. It has a trader’s charm to it: warm, alert, and hard to read from only one angle.
flavorText: “Tiger eye does not stare. It appraises.”

name: topaz ring
shortDescription: A topaz ring with a clear golden gleam, cheerful without becoming foolish. It suits summer courts, prosperous guildwives, and merchants who like their good fortune set where everyone can see it.
flavorText: “Topaz is sunlight that agreed to sit still.”

name: scarce tourmaline ring
shortDescription: A scarce tourmaline ring, colored with the complicated beauty that makes jewelers lean closer before speaking. Its charm lies in not being one simple thing, which also explains most of its price.
flavorText: “Tourmaline changes its mind beautifully.”

name: rough wooden ring
shortDescription: A rough wooden ring, carved simply and smoothed by wear rather than luxury. It may be a pilgrim token, a lover’s poor promise, or the quiet work of someone with a knife, a branch, and something to remember.
flavorText: “Wooden rings do not last forever; that is why some vows keep them safe.”

name: black pepper
shortDescription: Black pepper is a sharp little spice with more power than its size suggests, dark, wrinkled, and wonderfully rude to bland food. Cooks want it, healers argue over it, and traders love that a small pouch can change a whole meal’s price.
flavorText: “A peppercorn is proof that tiny things can still bite.”

name: ginger
shortDescription: Ginger is a knotted root with bright heat inside its rough skin, equally welcome in kitchens, tonics, and cold-weather remedies. It smells clean, bites back, and travels better than many sweeter luxuries.
flavorText: “Ginger warms twice: once in the hand, once in the throat.”

name: salt
shortDescription: Salt is plain enough to be overlooked and important enough to start arguments across kingdoms. It preserves meat, sharpens stew, cures hides, blesses thresholds, and reminds every trader that necessity is the oldest luxury.
flavorText: “Gold buys feasts, but salt keeps them from rotting.”

name: chives
shortDescription: Chives are slender green herbs with a mild onion bite, best sold fresh before their brightness tires. They make humble food look cared for, which is one of the cheapest forms of kitchen magic.
flavorText: “A little chive convinces soup it had a plan.”

name: thyme
shortDescription: Thyme is a small, woody herb with a deep savory scent that clings to fingers and memory. Cooks tuck it into roasts and stews, while healers keep it near because useful plants rarely mind having more than one job.
flavorText: “Thyme is patience made fragrant.”

name: garlic
shortDescription: Garlic is a blunt, loyal bulb that improves food, offends breath, and terrifies several kinds of folklore. It is cheap when common, priceless when missing, and never shy about making itself known.
flavorText: “Garlic is the cook’s hammer: crude, beloved, and often exactly right.”

name: cinnamon
shortDescription: Cinnamon curls in sweet brown strips, carrying warmth, dessert, medicine, and winter comfort in a bark thin enough to snap. A little makes porridge feel wealthy; a lot announces that someone expected guests.
flavorText: “Cinnamon is how a kitchen apologizes for cold weather.”

name: parsley
shortDescription: Parsley is a bright green herb with clean flavor and the useful habit of making plates look less desperate. It is common, quick to wilt, and loved by cooks who know freshness can be seen before it is tasted.
flavorText: “Parsley is the last-minute honesty of a good cook.”

name: sage
shortDescription: Sage is a gray-green herb with a deep, almost serious scent, suited to rich meats, old remedies, and rooms that need clearing after too much bad news. It tastes like memory when used well and like punishment when used carelessly.
flavorText: “Sage has the flavor of advice you were not ready to hear.”

name: mint
shortDescription: Mint is a cool, quick herb that smells like shade beside water and leaves the mouth feeling newly awake. It is bought for teas, sweets, sauces, and the small mercy of freshness after heavy meals.
flavorText: “Mint is the garden’s way of laughing at heat.”

name: mustard
shortDescription: Mustard brings a bright, sharp heat that wakes dull food and surprises the unwary nose. Ground, crushed, or stirred into sauce, it has the useful merchant virtue of seeming modest until opened.
flavorText: “Mustard waits politely, then interrupts.”

name: basil
shortDescription: Basil is a tender green herb with a sweet peppery scent, beloved in warm kitchens and impatient markets. It bruises easily, which only makes good bundles more valuable before the day’s heat takes them.
flavorText: “Basil is summer pretending it can fit in a basket.”

name: hot pepper
shortDescription: Hot pepper is bright, dangerous, and often underestimated by people who trust color more than experience. It can rescue a dull stew, ruin a boastful diner, and make even a cheap meal feel like a wager.
flavorText: “A hot pepper is a small red lesson in humility.”

name: oregano
shortDescription: Oregano is a sturdy herb with a warm, slightly wild scent, good for flatbreads, sauces, roasted vegetables, and food meant to travel in memory. It has the practical charm of hillside kitchens and busy hands.
flavorText: “Oregano tastes like someone remembered the road home.”

name: rosemary
shortDescription: Rosemary is a resinous sprig with needle leaves and a scent that survives heat, smoke, and forgetful cooks. It belongs with roasted meat, travel bread, and old households where memory is treated like seasoning.
flavorText: “Rosemary stays after the meal, which is why people call it remembrance.”

name: cloves
shortDescription: Cloves are dark little spikes with a sweet heat strong enough to rule a whole pot from the corner. Bakers, perfumers, dentists, and winter merchants all know their value rises when the air turns cold.
flavorText: “A clove is a nail the kitchen drives into memory.”

name: notable nutmeg
shortDescription: Notable nutmeg is a prized whole spice with a warm, rounded fragrance hidden under a hard brown shell. A careful grating can sweeten custards, sauces, and wine; a careless one announces extravagance before the dish arrives.
flavorText: “Nutmeg is the spice of people who own a tiny grater and opinions.”

name: notable licorice root
shortDescription: Notable licorice root is tough, fibrous, and surprisingly sweet when chewed or steeped. Apothecaries prize it for syrups, cooks for strange confections, and children for the delightful suspicion that medicine might be tricked into tasting good.
flavorText: “Licorice root is proof that sweetness sometimes arrives dressed as a stick.”
